Title: Transport of defense compounds from source to sink: lessons learned from glucosinolates

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mediates glucosinolate import and is essential for proper long-distance transport to seeds, retention/storage in roots, and proper intra-leaf glucosinolate distribution; loss contributes to glucosinolate-free seeds in the atgtr1 atgtr2 mutant.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane; phloem companion cells; mesophyll cells in rosette leaves; cortex cells, pericycle, and root vasculature in roots
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense against herbivores and pathogens; shaping tissue-specific defense and possibly rhizosphere interactions via breakdown products
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seeds (embryo), roots (especially cortex), leaf margins, abaxial epidermis, S-cells along vasculature
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in leaf phloem companion cells and mesophyll; in roots predominantly in cortex cells and root vasculature; induced by jasmonic acid treatment
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in leaf phloem companion cells and mesophyll; in roots in cortex/pericycle/vasculature; relevant during source–sink transport to seeds and within leaves
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Xenopus laevis oocyte uptake assay; Arabidopsis atgtr1 mutant and atgtr1 atgtr2 double-mutant phenotyping; glucosinolate profiling; micrografting; tissue localization/expression analyses; feeding studies
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Developmental transition from vegetative to reproductive growth; biotic stress context; jasmonic acid treatment; continuous light for fertility phenotype discussed in relation to possible hormone transport
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtGTR1 was identified as a plasma membrane glucosinolate transporter by oocyte uptake assays. 2. Together with AtGTR2, it is essential for transport of aliphatic and indole glucosinolates to seeds, as double mutants have seed glucosinolates below detection. 3. AtGTR1 additionally functions in mesophyll-associated intra-leaf import and contributes to root glucosinolate retention/distribution. 4. Loss of GTR function alters source–sink relations, causing redistribution/overaccumulation of specific glucosinolates in leaves and whole-plant glucosinolate changes.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible reduced fertility under continuous light reported for atgtr1 and proposed multifunctionality in phytohormone transport; altered glucosinolate distribution may perturb defense allocation
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Functional genomics screen of Arabidopsis transporter library in Xenopus oocytes; large-scale root cell-type metabolomics referenced; accession numbers not provided
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for engineering tissue-specific glucosinolate allocation, including reduction of seed glucosinolates or manipulation of root/shoot defense distribution; should consider effects on whole-plant source–sink relations and possible fertility/hormone-related pleiotropy
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review article. AtGTR1 is presented as a core summarized transporter with primary evidence from prior studies. The review notes that AtGTR1 may also transport JA-Ile/GA3 in vitro, but its primary demonstrated in planta role is glucosinolate transport.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mediates glucosinolate import and, together with AtGTR1, is essential for long-distance transport of glucosinolates to seeds, retention/storage in roots, and proper intra-leaf distribution.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane; phloem companion cells in leaves; cortex cells and root phloem companion cells/vasculature in roots
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense against herbivores and pathogens; establishment of dynamic source–sink defense allocation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seeds (embryo), roots, leaf margins, epidermis, vascular-associated S-cells
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in leaf phloem companion cells; in roots strongly in cortex cells and also root phloem companion cells
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in leaf phloem companion cells and root cortex/phloem-associated tissues during glucosinolate translocation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Xenopus laevis oocyte uptake assay; Arabidopsis atgtr1 atgtr2 mutant phenotyping; glucosinolate profiling; micrografting; tissue localization/expression analyses; feeding studies
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Developmental changes in source–sink status; biotic stress context
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtGTR2 was identified as a glucosinolate transporter by oocyte uptake assays. 2. Together with AtGTR1, it is indispensable for seed loading of both aliphatic and indole glucosinolates. 3. Root expression suggests functions in phloem loading and intraroot distribution/retention of glucosinolates. 4. Loss of GTR1/GTR2 changes glucosinolate partitioning between roots, rosette, and reproductive tissues.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered whole-plant glucosinolate allocation in mutants may affect defense deployment and metabolic balance
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Functional genomics screen of Arabidopsis transporter library in Xenopus oocytes; referenced metabolomics studies without accession numbers
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for modifying glucosinolate partitioning among seeds, leaves, and roots in Brassicaceae; deployment should consider strong redistribution effects and possible compensation in biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review article summarizing prior work. AtGTR2 is emphasized as a core transporter but no unique independent role beyond partnership with AtGTR1 is firmly established here.
